channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Tides of Tomorrow Preview - Multiple Paths Through the Same Story
item.content:encoded:
Tides of Tomorrow is a narrative focused RPG with a unique multiplayer spin. Instead of experiencing the story one way, you follow in the footsteps of someone who played before you. Their choices change how the world sees you, for better or worse. Faced with an incurable disease, medicine shortages, and a religious cult, the game has layers of story to swim through. This is how we felt after experiencing the life of a Tidewalker in our Tides of Tomorrow preview. 

Tides of Tomorrow Preview | A Plastic Future 

The narrative of Tides of Tomorrow centers around the Tidewalkers, fabled beings from before the world flooded. Gifted with the ability to see what other Tidewalkers did before them, they are prophesized to bring about the cure for Plastemia, an illness that slowly turns the human body to plastic. This ability is the main mechanic of the game. By looking into the past, you can see where the person you're following walked, what they interacted with, where they hid, etc. 

People also react to you differently. If the person before you caused trouble for Obin, the guards will be more alert. You may be completely locked out of a city and have to sneak through the vents if someone ticked Obin off enough. If you win a race in Pleasureland, your followers will have an easier time making friends and getting to see Nyx. You can play multiple times and pick a new path, a new type of person to follow, each time. Not only that, but if you don't like the decisions of the Tidewalker before you, you have the ability to pick someone new whenever you travel to a new location.

Visions of the Past and Holding Hands

While Tides of Tomorrow’s ability to look into the past and changing narrative gives it a unique spin, it also presents some challenges. The main problem is trying not to feel like the game is holding your hand throughout. Looking into the Tides of the Past can give you a plethora of answers, some needed to move forward. The visions are part of the game's fabric and cannot be avoided completely.

Even so, looking to the past can absolutely ruin the excitement of discovering the way through the game yourself. Even though fellow Tidewalkers can make your adventure harder, seeing where they went, what they did, and what choices they made leans towards hand holding. I found myself resorting less and less on the Tides of the Past as I played, opting to explore on my own.

Another perk of a changing narrative based on who you follow is being able to replay the game multiple times. You can explore areas you missed, be presented with completely new scenes, and retry events you may have failed before. The downside is that there is no way to skip cutscenes or speed through dialogue. You’ll have to rewatch and re-experience multiple parts of the game, whether you want to or not.

There’s a lot going on visually in Tides of Tomorrow. The design is detailed, but can be difficult for those with vision triggered headaches or migraines to even look at. Plastemia, which affects you more the less health you have, causes blurred vision, distorted lighting, and general unstableness. If you’re like me, the bright, blurry colors and flashes can trigger your migraines. Be wary of playing if your head is already pounding.

Overall, Tides of Tomorrow is a notable RPG to experience. It's visually diverse, the world is filled with interesting characters, and the story can get its hooks into you. Giving it a once through is a must for story enthusiasts. Playing it multiple times can be a hassle, however, that can reduce how much of the narrative you actually experience. If you don't mind revisiting scenes you can't skip and being led through some of the gameplay, Tides of Tomorrow may be the perfect match for you.

Tides of Tomorrow was previewed on PC over the course of 5 and 1/2 hours with a code provided by the publisher. All screenshots were taken during the course of the review.

Ashley Erickson is an experienced writer focusing on the video games industry since 2013. Starting off with cosplay features, she moved to game guides and… More about Ashley



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/tides-of-tomorrow-preview-multiple-paths-through-same-story
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Knights of the Old Republic Remake Still Happening, Saber Says
item.content:encoded:
If, like many of us, you were starting to believe that the long-awaited Knights of the Old Republic remake was dead and gone, then Saber Interactive's Tim Willits has some news for you: it's actually still in development.

In a recent interview with IGN, Willits confirmed that the game is still in the works, but that the fact its existence continues is "all [he] can say". It's not much, but for now, it seems as though the project isn't dead, at the very least.

The Knights of the Old Republic remake was originally revealed all the way back in September 2021 as part of a PlayStation showcase, and at the time, porting studio Aspyr Media was set to take the reins on the project.

Following the project's announcement, the Knights of the Old Republic remake was delayed indefinitely in the summer of 2022, and in August that same year, Aspyr was taken off the project officially, with Saber Interactive stepping up to take over development.

Fears began to mount again in 2023, however, when Sony and Saber's then-parent company Embracer began scrubbing all evidence of the KotOR remake from the internet, including trailers and social media posts.

Since then, we haven't heard anything official about the remake (well, until today, at least), and Saber hasn't released any new trailers, nor have we seen any gameplay footage.

As such, I'd be inclined to take Willits' comments with a pinch of salt; it may well be that the Knights of the Old Republic remake remains on Saber's slate for the time being, but the gaming industry being as volatile as it is at the moment, I wouldn't bank on that staying the case forever.

In the meantime, you'll have to content yourself with Arcanaut Studios' spiritual successor Fate of the Old Republic, which was announced during last year's Game Awards. Development is being led by ex-BioWare luminary Casey Hudson, too, although again, we haven't seen any gameplay yet.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/knights-of-the-old-republic-remake-still-happening-saber
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Xbox's Next-Gen Console Is Codenamed Project Helix, and It'll Play PC Games
item.content:encoded:
Xbox has revealed Project Helix, the codename for its next generation of console hardware, and according to new Xbox head Asha Sharma, it'll play your PC games as well.

The new hardware generation was revealed on social media with a very simple and short video that depicts Project Helix's logo, and, well, that's pretty much all we got from Xbox itself; no first look at what the hardware will look like, no game lineup, nothing else.

New Xbox CEO Asha Sharma did provide a little more info over on her social media, however, promising that Project Helix will "lead in performance" and that it will "play your Xbox and PC games". That's not much, but it's a bit of extra context, at the very least.

Sharma says she will talk more about Project Helix "with partners and studios at [her] first GDC next week", although how much of that conversation will be released to the public remains to be seen. I don't expect we'll hear much about what Project Helix actually is for quite some time.

Project Helix's announcement comes amid a trying time for Xbox in the hardware stakes. While Sony's PlayStation 5 goes from strength to strength, Xbox hardware sales are floundering, and Xbox has pivoted to a more software-focused approach that sees its catalog becoming available on other devices.

This isn't the first time we've heard that next-generation Xbox hardware will take a broader view of the market, either; back in June last year, now-former exec Sarah Bond announced that next-gen Xbox consoles wouldn't be tied to a single storefront or a single device.

With that in mind, it looks like Project Helix will move away from the traditional console experience favored by Xbox's last few hardware releases in favor of something we haven't seen before.

It's likely going to be a while before anything more pops up about Project Helix. Stay tuned for more on this and all other things Xbox-related.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/xbox-project-helix-reveal-next-gen
